description: Guidance for locating ancestors who 'disappeared' into institutions — poorhouses, asylums, orphanages, prisons, hospitals, and work camps — with sensitivity guidance for presenting institutionalization to descendants

patterns.scenario: An ancestor disappears from census and tax records, and you suspect poverty or disability led to institutionalization
patterns.era_scope: 1800-1950, peak poorhouse era 1830-1920 before modern welfare programs replaced them
patterns.interpretation: Poorhouses (also called almshouses, county farms, or county homes) housed the destitute, elderly, disabled, mentally ill, and chronically ill. They were operated by counties and existed in virtually every American county from the early 1800s through the mid-1900s. Admission records typically include name, age, birthplace, occupation, reason for admission (infirmity, old age, insanity, blindness, etc.), and date of admission. These records are a critical source for ancestors who fell through the cracks of more visible documentation.
action_steps: Contact the county archives or county clerk for poorhouse/almshouse admission and discharge records — many counties transferred these records to the state archives when the institutions closed. The county historical society may also hold them

Search the census for the institutional population — poorhouse residents were enumerated on separate census pages within the county's enumeration district. Browse the census pages for the poorhouse in the relevant county. On Ancestry, search for the ancestor with 'relationship' set to 'inmate' or 'patient'

Check county financial records — county commissioners' minutes often include lists of poorhouse admissions, expenditures for 'outdoor relief' (financial aid without institutionalization), and burial records for residents who died at the institution

Search the poorhouse cemetery records — many poorhouse residents were buried on the grounds in pauper graves, sometimes with numbered markers rather than names. Some counties maintained burial registers that survived even when admission records did not. FindAGrave volunteers have documented some of these cemeteries

For the period after 1935, check Social Security records — the transition from poorhouses to Social Security created documentation as elderly poorhouse residents applied for benefits. Their SS-5 applications may be the only record with their parents' names
common_mistakes: Assuming poorhouse residence means moral failing — people entered poorhouses because of old age, disability, mental illness, spousal death, economic depression, or workplace injury. The institution was a social safety net, not a punishment
Not looking for the poorhouse in the census — institutional residents are enumerated but often on pages separate from the general population and may not appear in name indexes
Ignoring county commissioners' minutes, which document individual admissions and expenditures and may provide details not in the admission register

patterns.scenario: An ancestor may have been committed to a state hospital for mental illness
patterns.era_scope: 1840-1980, the era of large state-run psychiatric institutions
patterns.interpretation: State mental hospitals (asylums, state hospitals, state institutions) housed hundreds of thousands of Americans from the mid-1800s through deinstitutionalization in the 1960s-1980s. Admission records typically include detailed biographical information: name, age, birthplace, occupation, marital status, next of kin, and a narrative description of the patient's condition and history. Access to these records varies enormously by state — some have opened historical records, others restrict them for 75-100 years or require proof of kinship.
action_steps: Contact the state archives to determine what institutional records survive and what access policies apply — each state has different rules. New York, Massachusetts, Ohio, and Pennsylvania have relatively accessible historical asylum records. Some states have transferred records to the state archives; others retain them at the facility or the state health department

Check the commitment records at the county courthouse — commitment to a state hospital required a court order, and the commitment hearing record (including physician testimony, family petitions, and the court's finding) is part of the county court record. These are public records in most states

Search the census for the institution — state hospital patients were enumerated, typically on separate sheets listing name, age, birthplace, and condition. Browse the census pages for the known institutions in the state

Look for the patient in the institution's published annual reports — many state hospitals published annual reports to the legislature that included admission and discharge statistics, sometimes with patient lists or case summaries. These are held at state libraries and on Google Books or HathiTrust

For deceased patients, check the institution's burial records — state hospitals maintained their own cemeteries for patients who died without family to claim the body. These records may be at the state archives, the facility, or the state health department
common_mistakes: Assuming mental hospital records are completely sealed — while living-patient records are restricted, historical records (typically 75+ years old) are available in many states, and commitment records at the county courthouse are generally public
Not checking county court records for the commitment hearing, which is often more accessible than the hospital's own records and contains significant biographical and medical detail

Applying modern diagnostic categories to 19th-century commitments — 'moral insanity,' 'puerperal insanity' (postpartum), 'masturbational insanity,' and 'religious excitement' were recognized diagnoses that reflected the era's understanding, not the patient's actual condition

patterns.scenario: An ancestor was placed in an orphanage as a child, possibly through the orphan train program or local placement
patterns.era_scope: 1850-1960, the institutional orphan care era; orphan trains 1854-1929
patterns.interpretation: Orphanages housed not only true orphans (both parents dead) but also 'half-orphans' (one parent dead), children of families in crisis (poverty, illness, incarceration), and abandoned children. The Orphan Train Movement (1854-1929) placed an estimated 250,000 children from Northeastern cities with families in the Midwest and West. Orphanage records are typically held by the institution or its successor organization, not by government archives.
action_steps: Identify the specific orphanage — use census records (the child may be enumerated in the institution), county records (a county court may have ordered placement), or family knowledge. County histories and local histories often describe which orphanages served the area

Contact the institution or its successor — many orphanages were operated by religious organizations (Catholic, Protestant, Jewish) and their records may be with the diocesan archives, the denominational historical society, or a successor social services agency. The National Orphan Train Complex (orphantrainriders.com) in Concordia, Kansas maintains records and a research center for orphan train placements

For orphan train children, search the Children's Aid Society records (now held at the New-York Historical Society) and similar placing-out agencies. The records include the child's name, parents' information (if known), placing-out details, and sometimes follow-up reports on the child's placement

Check the New York Foundling Hospital records (for Catholic orphan train children) — the Foundling Hospital placed approximately 25,000 children, primarily in Catholic families in the Midwest. Records are at the New York Foundling archives

Search county court records for guardianship, adoption, and indenture records — when a child left an orphanage to a family, the legal transfer was recorded at the county courthouse. Indenture records (binding a child to a family for labor and training) continued into the early 1900s
common_mistakes: Assuming orphanage children were truly orphaned — many had at least one living parent. The orphanage was often a temporary placement during family crisis, and the child may have been reclaimed by a parent later
Not searching census records for the institution — the orphanage census enumeration is often the best way to confirm institutional placement and to find the child's reported age and birthplace
Giving up when an institution has closed — records were typically transferred to a successor organization, the parent religious body, or a state or county archives
source_types_to_check: Children's Aid Society records (New-York Historical Society)

patterns.scenario: An ancestor was incarcerated in a state or federal prison
patterns.interpretation: Prison intake records are among the most detailed physical descriptions of any ancestor: height, weight, eye color, hair color, complexion, scars, tattoos, and sometimes a photograph (mug shots from the 1850s onward). They also include birthplace, occupation, education level, marital status, next of kin, and the crime and sentence. For ancestors who left few other records, prison files may be the most complete biographical document that survives.
action_steps: Contact the state archives for state prison records — most states have transferred historical prison records to the state archives, typically with a 50-75 year access delay. Intake registers, discharge records, and pardon applications are the most common surviving record types

For federal prisons, search NARA Record Group 129 (Records of the Bureau of Prisons) — historical records from federal penitentiaries (Leavenworth, Atlanta, McNeil Island, Alcatraz) include inmate case files with detailed biographical information. Some indexes are on Ancestry

Search the county criminal court records at the county courthouse — the trial record (indictment, witness testimony, verdict, sentence) is separate from the prison record and may contain different information. County court records are generally public

Check for pardon and commutation records at the state archives or governor's office — pardon applications include petitions from the prisoner, family members, community members, and sometimes the trial judge. These contain biographical detail and character assessments

Search Ancestry's state and federal prison databases — Ancestry has digitized prison intake registers for several states including California, Illinois, Ohio, Pennsylvania, and others. Search by name, and browse by institution and date
common_mistakes: Assuming prison records are sealed or inaccessible — historical records (generally 50+ years old) are available at state archives in most states. Current records are restricted, but genealogical-era records are typically open
Not searching county court records for the trial itself — the prison only documents incarceration, while the court record documents the events leading to conviction

Hiding or omitting incarceration from the family narrative — descendants have a right to accurate family history. Incarceration can be presented factually and with context (see sensitivity guidance below)

patterns.scenario: An ancestor was hospitalized for an extended period, possibly for tuberculosis, a chronic illness, or a work injury
patterns.era_scope: 1870-1950, the era of extended hospitalization before antibiotics and modern treatments shortened stays
patterns.interpretation: Long-term hospitalization was common for tuberculosis (sanatoriums), polio, chronic illness, and industrial injuries. Hospital records from this era include admission details (name, age, address, next of kin, occupation, employer), diagnosis, treatment notes, and discharge or death record. Access varies: most hospital records are protected by medical privacy laws, but historical records (75-100+ years old) may be available through the hospital, its successor, or the state archives.
action_steps: Identify the hospital — use death certificates (which typically name the hospital), city directories, and family knowledge. County histories describe which hospitals served an area

Contact the hospital's medical records department or, if the hospital has closed, the successor institution or the state health department — explain that you are seeking genealogical information from historical records (75+ years old) and ask about their access policy for historical patient records

For tuberculosis sanatoriums, check the state archives — many state-run TB sanatoriums transferred their records to the state archives when they closed in the 1950s-1960s. These records are often accessible for genealogical research
Search the census for hospital patients — hospitalized individuals were enumerated at the institution. Browse the census pages for the relevant hospital in the enumeration district

For veterans' hospitals, request records through the VA using SF-180 (next of kin only for deceased veterans) — VA hospital records include admission dates, diagnosis, treatment, and discharge disposition
common_mistakes: Assuming all hospital records are confidential — HIPAA applies to current records, not records from 100 years ago. Access policies vary by institution, but many have accommodated genealogical requests for historical records
Not checking the death certificate for the hospital name — this is the simplest way to identify where the ancestor was hospitalized
Overlooking county hospital records — county-run hospitals (often called 'county general' or 'charity hospital') served the poor and uninsured and may have records at the county archives

patterns.scenario: A male ancestor aged 18-25 during the 1933-1942 period may have been enrolled in the Civilian Conservation Corps
patterns.interpretation: The Civilian Conservation Corps enrolled approximately 3 million young men (ages 18-25, later expanded to 17-28) during the Great Depression. CCC enrollment records include the enrollee's name, date of birth, place of birth, home address, next of kin, physical examination results, and camp assignment. The CCC provides a location record for young men during the Depression years when they might otherwise be difficult to trace.
action_steps: Request CCC enrollment records from the National Personnel Records Center (NPRC) in St. Louis using Standard Form 180 — CCC records are in the civilian personnel files section. Include the enrollee's full name, approximate date of birth, and home state
Search the CCC Legacy Foundation database (ccclegacy.org) for camp locations, company histories, and some enrollee information

Check state-level CCC records at the state archives — some states maintained their own enrollment and camp records independently of the federal system. State-level records may be more accessible than NARA records

Search for the ancestor in the 1940 census, which may show them at a CCC camp — CCC camps were enumerated as separate locations. The camp location appears in the census as a place name within a county

Look for CCC camp newspapers — many camps published their own newsletters with enrollee names, activities, and sometimes photographs. These survive at state archives, local historical societies, and the National Archives
common_mistakes: Not knowing that CCC records exist at NPRC — they are frequently overlooked because the CCC was a civilian program, not military, and researchers do not think to request them through the military records process
Assuming all CCC enrollees were at camps far from home — while some were sent to distant states, many served at camps within their home state or region
Not checking for multiple enrollments — enrollees could serve up to 2 years (in six-month increments), and some re-enrolled after a break

patterns.scenario: You have confirmed that an ancestor was in a poorhouse, asylum, prison, or other institution and need to present this information to descendants
patterns.era_scope: All eras — this is a narrative presentation concern, not a records concern
patterns.interpretation: Institutionalization carries social stigma, and descendants may have complex feelings about learning that an ancestor was in a poorhouse, asylum, or prison. However, honest family history requires including these facts. The key is context: explain the circumstances that led to institutionalization (economic depression, workplace injury, spousal death, mental illness without modern treatment options) and the social systems of the era (poorhouses as the only safety net, asylums as the standard treatment, incarceration practices different from today).
action_steps: Present the facts clearly and without euphemism — do not hide the institutionalization, but provide the historical context that explains it. 'John entered the county poorhouse in 1893 after a mining injury left him unable to work' is honest and compassionate

Explain the institution's role in its era — poorhouses served as nursing homes, disability support, and homeless shelters combined. Asylums treated conditions that are now managed with medication. Prisons confined people under laws and practices very different from today

Note the systemic factors — economic depression, workplace danger without safety regulation or workers' compensation, disease without antibiotics, disability without social insurance, and mental illness without effective treatment were systemic conditions, not personal failures

If the ancestor was institutionalized for something now recognized as unjust (committed to an asylum for 'female hysteria,' imprisoned under Jim Crow laws, confined for debt), state this clearly as a historical injustice

Include the ancestor's full life arc, not just the institutional period — show what came before and after. Many people left institutions and rebuilt their lives. The institutional period is one chapter, not the whole story
common_mistakes: Omitting the institutionalization entirely to 'protect' the family's reputation — this erases part of the ancestor's life and deprives descendants of their full history
Presenting institutionalization as shameful or sensational rather than as a factual part of the life story
Not providing historical context, which leaves the reader to project modern assumptions onto historical situations
source_types_to_check: county and state histories for institutional context
historical studies of poorhouses, asylums, and prisons
published social welfare histories for the relevant era

source_references: Wagner, David. 'The Poorhouse: America's Forgotten Institution.' Rowman & Littlefield, 2005.
Grob, Gerald N. 'The Mad Among Us: A History of the Care of America's Mentally Ill.' Free Press, 1994.
O'Brien, Patrick. 'Orphan Trains to Missouri.' University of Missouri Press, 1997.
National Orphan Train Complex. 'Research Resources.' orphantrainriders.com.
National Archives. 'Records of the Civilian Conservation Corps (Record Group 35).' archives.gov.
